[1/5] Source: git-inceptionlabs | Status: completed | Part 1/2
Homemade Pizza – Simple Step‑by‑Step Guide
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12‑inch pizzas)
Ingredient	Amount
Bread‑flour (high‑protein)	500 g (≈ 4 cups)
Water (lukewarm, ~38 °C)	320 ml (≈ 1 ⅓ cups)
Active‑dry yeast	5 g (1 tsp)
Fine sea salt	10 g (2 tsp)
Olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Optional: a pinch of sugar	2 g (½ tsp)
Method
Activate yeast – In a small bowl, dissolve the yeast (and sugar, if using) in the warm water. Let sit 5 min until foamy.
Mix dry – In a large bowl, whisk flour and salt together.
Combine – Make a well in the flour, pour in the yeast mixture and olive oil. Stir with a wooden spoon until a shaggy dough forms.
Knead – Turn onto a lightly floured surface and knead 8‑10 min (or 5 min in a stand‑mix with a dough hook on medium speed) until smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
First rise – Lightly oil a clean bowl, place the dough inside, cover with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let rise 1‑1½ h at room temperature, or until doubled in size.
Punch & divide – Deflate gently, split into 2 equal balls (or 3 for smaller pizzas). Shape each into a tight round, cover, and rest 15 min – this relaxes the gluten for easier stretching.
2. Sauce (quick & flavorful)
Ingredient	Amount
Canned whole‑peeled tomatoes (San Marzano)	400 g (1 can)
Extra‑virgin olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Fresh garlic, minced	2 cloves
Dried oregano	½ tsp
Dried basil	½ tsp
Pinch of sugar (optional)	–
Salt & pepper	to taste
Fresh basil leaves (optional)	a few, torn
Method
Blend – Crush the tomatoes by hand or pulse briefly in a food processor; keep a slightly chunky texture.
Season – Stir in olive oil, garlic, oregano, basil, sugar, salt, and pepper. Taste and adjust. No need to cook; the sauce will finish in the oven, preserving bright tomato flavor.
3. Toppings (keep it simple)
Cheese: Fresh mozzarella (sliced or torn) – 150‑200 g per pizza.
Optional extras: Pepperoni, sliced mushrooms, roasted red peppers, caramelized onions, fresh arugula, drizzle of olive oil, grated Parmesan.
Tip: If using watery mozzarella, pat dry with paper towels to avoid soggy crust.
4. Baking
Parameter	Recommendation
Oven type	Conventional or convection (no pizza stone required, but a stone or steel gives the best crust).
Pre‑heat temperature	250 °C (482 °F) – highest setting your oven allows (most home ovens max at 250 °C).
Pre‑heat time	At least 30 min with the stone/steel inside.
Baking surface	Pizza stone, steel, or upside‑down baking sheet.
Bake time	8‑12 min – watch for a golden‑brown crust and bubbling cheese.
Broiler	If you like extra char, switch to broiler for the last 1‑2 min (watch closely).
Procedure
Stretch dough – On a well‑floured surface, gently stretch each ball into a 12‑inch circle, leaving a slightly thicker edge for the crust.
Add sauce – Spoon ¼‑⅓ cup of sauce, spreading thinly, leaving the rim untouched.
Cheese & toppings – Distribute mozzarella evenly, then add desired toppings.
Slide onto stone – Use a well‑floured pizza peel (or the back of a baking sheet) to transfer the pizza.
Bake – Slide onto the pre‑heated stone/steel. Bake 8‑12 min, rotating halfway for even browning.
Finish – Remove, drizzle a little olive oil, sprinkle fresh basil or Parmesan, and let rest 2 min before slicing.
5. Practical Tips for a Great Home Pizza
Tip	Why it helps
Use high‑protein flour (bread flour or “00” pizza flour)	Gives stronger gluten, yielding a chewy yet airy crust.

[1/5] Source: git-inceptionlabs | Status: completed | Part 2/2
Cold‑ferment dough (24‑48 h in the fridge)	Develops deeper flavor and a crisper crust.
Pre‑heat stone/steel thoroughly	Mimics a brick oven’s instant heat, producing a blistered “leopard‑spot” crust.
Don’t overload sauce/toppings	Keeps the crust crisp; excess moisture makes it soggy.
Bake at the highest temperature	Fast bake creates a puffy edge and prevents drying out the toppings.
Use a pizza peel dusted with semolina	Prevents sticking and adds a subtle crunch.
Rotate the pizza	Ensures even browning, especially in ovens with hot spots.

[3/5] Source: git-grok | Status: completed
Homemade Pizza at Home (Simple, Reliable Method)
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12–14" pizzas)
Ingredients (basic no-knead or quick version):
500 g (4 cups) bread flour or all-purpose flour
300–325 ml (1¼–1⅓ cups) lukewarm water
2 tsp instant yeast (or 7 g packet)
2 tsp salt
2 tbsp olive oil
Quick steps:
Mix dry ingredients (flour, yeast, salt).
Add water + olive oil. Stir until shaggy dough forms (no heavy kneading needed).
Cover and let rise 1–2 hours at room temp (or overnight in fridge for better flavor).
Divide into 2 balls, rest 15–20 min, then stretch by hand into thin rounds (don't use rolling pin if possible — it deflates air pockets).
Tip: For crispier crust, use bread flour and let dough cold-ferment in fridge 24–48 hrs.
2. Sauce (simple classic)
1 can (400 g / 14 oz) crushed tomatoes or passata
1–2 garlic cloves, minced
1 tsp dried oregano
½ tsp salt
Pinch of sugar + black pepper
Optional: fresh basil, red pepper flakes
Mix everything cold — no cooking needed for fresh flavor. Spread thinly (too much sauce makes soggy pizza).
3. Assembly & Toppings
Preheat oven as hot as it goes (ideally 475–550°F / 245–290°C). Use a pizza stone or steel if you have one; otherwise a heavy baking sheet flipped upside-down.
Stretch dough on floured parchment or semolina.
Add thin layer of sauce, then cheese (fresh mozzarella torn into pieces + low-moisture mozzarella for melt), then toppings.
Practical tip: Less is more — overload = soggy. Add delicate items (basil, prosciutto) after baking.
4. Baking
Temperature: 475–550°F (245–290°C) — the hotter the better for home ovens.
Timing:
With pizza stone/steel: 8–12 minutes (rotate halfway).
On regular baking sheet: 12–15 minutes.
Pizza is done when crust is golden-brown with some char spots and cheese is bubbling.
Best home hack: Preheat stone/sheet for 45–60 min at max temp. Launch pizza on parchment for first 5 min, then slide parchment out so bottom crisps directly on stone.
Practical Tips for Success
Crust issues: Too thick? Stretch thinner next time. Soggy bottom? Hotter oven + preheat longer + less sauce.
Cheese: Mix fresh mozzarella (flavor) with low-moisture shredded (less water release).
Oven variation: Gas vs electric matters — watch the first pizza closely and adjust.
Make-ahead: Dough and sauce keep 2–3 days in fridge. Freeze dough balls after first rise.
No stone? Use cast-iron skillet or grill (outdoor grill at high heat gives great results).
Scale toppings to your taste: Margherita (sauce, mozzarella, basil, olive oil drizzle after bake) is the easiest winner.
Start simple with Margherita for your first try — it teaches you the fundamentals fast. Once comfortable, experiment with toppings.
Enjoy your pizza night!

[5/5] Source: git-perplexity | Status: completed | Part 1/2
Here’s a straightforward, practical guide to make pizza at home with dough, sauce, bake temps, timing, and tips.
Dough (yields 2 medium pizzas)
Ingredients: 2 cups bread flour (or all-purpose), 1 cup warm water (105–110°F/40–43°C), 1 teaspoon instant or active dry yeast, 1 teaspoon sugar, 1 teaspoon salt, 1 tablespoon olive oil.
Method:
Combine water, yeast, and sugar; let sit 5 minutes if using active dry yeast to proof. If using instant yeast, you can mix directly.
Mix in flour and salt, then knead until smooth and elastic (about 6–8 minutes by hand, 4–6 with a mixer). Add a little flour if sticky; add a touch of water if dry.
Coat dough with a little oil, cover, and rise 60–90 minutes at room temp, or until doubled.
Divide into 2 balls, flatten into discs, and let rest 15–20 minutes before stretching.
Tips: For a crispier crust, use bread flour or a 60–40 mix with whole-wheat, and aim for a dough hydration around 65–70%.
Pizza Sauce (quick no-cook version)
Ingredients: 1 can (14–15 oz) crushed tomatoes or tomato puree, 1–2 cloves garlic (minced) or 1 teaspoon garlic powder, 1–2 tablespoons olive oil, 1 teaspoon dried oregano, ½ teaspoon dried basil, ½ teaspoon sugar (optional), salt and pepper to taste.
Method:
Blend or whisk together tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, oregano, basil, sugar, and seasonings until smooth.
Adjust with salt, pepper, or a touch more olive oil to taste.
Quick no-cook note: If you prefer, blend tomatoes with a small amount of olive oil and seasonings; spread thinly on dough if you’re avoiding pre-cooking.
Tips: Use a thin layer of sauce to prevent soggy crust; sauce can be stored in the fridge 3–5 days or frozen for later.
Bake temperature and timing
Home oven (conventional): Preheat to 475–500°F (245–260°C). Bake 10–12 minutes for a thin crust, 12–15 minutes for a standard crust, until edges are golden and cheese is bubbling.
Pizza stone or steel: Place in the cold oven and preheat with the stone/steel inside for 30–45 minutes, then slide pizza onto it for crisper bottom crust.
For very hot setups (optional): If you have a grill or a dedicated pizza oven that reaches 600–900°F, cooking times drop to 2–6 minutes depending on thickness.
Tips: If crust browns too quickly, reduce oven temperature by 25°F and bake a bit longer; if underdone, give it a few more minutes.
Topping assembly
Base: Stretch or roll dough to 12-inch (30 cm) circle; transfer to a floured peel or parchment on a baking surface.
Sauce: Spread a thin layer (1–2 tablespoons per 12-inch dough) to avoid soggy crust.
Cheese: Classic mozzarella (shredded or sliced) or a blend with provolone; sprinkle salt sparingly.
Extras: Pepperoni, vegetables, mushrooms, olives, fresh basil, and a light drizzle of olive oil after baking.
Practical tips
Hydration: Aim for a dough that’s tacky but not sticky; adjust flour by 1–2 tablespoons as needed.
Kneading: Proper gluten development yields a chewy crust; you should be able to poke dough and have it slowly spring back.
Resting: A 15–20 minute bench rest helps dough relax and stretch better.
Pre-bake test: For thick-crust pies, you can par-bake dough 4–5 minutes before adding sauce and toppings to prevent sogginess.
Storage: On dough: refrigerate for up to 3 days or freeze for longer. Sauce can be made in larger batches and frozen.

[5/5] Source: git-perplexity | Status: completed | Part 2/2
Tools: A pizza stone or steel, a wide rolling pin or your hands for shaping, and a heat-safe peel or inverted baking sheet to transfer.
Example quarter-by-quarter timing (two 12-inch pizzas)
0–15 minutes: Mix, knead, and first rise.
15–105 minutes: Divide, shape, and rest dough.
105–120 minutes: Preheat oven/stone to 475–500°F.
120–132 minutes: Assemble toppings on dough.
132–144 minutes: Bake until crust is golden and cheese is melted.
If you want, tell me what equipment you have (oven type, stone/steel, or grill) and your preferred crust style (thin, standard, or thick), and I’ll tailor temps, times, and dough hydration to match.

Storage: On dough: refrigerate for up to 3 days or freeze for longer. Sauce can be made in larger batches and frozen.

Tools: A pizza stone or steel, a wide rolling pin or your hands for shaping, and a heat-safe peel or inverted baking sheet to transfer.

Example quarter-by-quarter timing (two 12-inch pizzas)

0–15 minutes: Mix, knead, and first rise.

15–105 minutes: Divide, shape, and rest dough.

105–120 minutes: Preheat oven/stone to 475–500°F.

120–132 minutes: Assemble toppings on dough.

132–144 minutes: Bake until crust is golden and cheese is melted.

If you want, tell me what equipment you have (oven type, stone/steel, or grill) and your preferred crust style (thin, standard, or thick), and I’ll tailor temps, times, and dough hydration to match.

Homemade Pizza – Simple Step‑by‑Step Guide
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12‑inch pizzas)
Ingredient	Amount
Bread‑flour (high‑protein)	500 g (≈ 4 cups)
Water (lukewarm, ~38 °C)	320 ml (≈ 1 ⅓ cups)
Active‑dry yeast	5 g (1 tsp)
Fine sea salt	10 g (2 tsp)
Olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Optional: a pinch of sugar	2 g (½ tsp)
Method
Activate yeast – In a small bowl, dissolve the yeast (and sugar, if using) in the warm water. Let sit 5 min until foamy.
Mix dry – In a large bowl, whisk flour and salt together.
Combine – Make a well in the flour, pour in the yeast mixture and olive oil. Stir with a wooden spoon until a shaggy dough forms.
Knead – Turn onto a lightly floured surface and knead 8‑10 min (or 5 min in a stand‑mix with a dough hook on medium speed) until smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
First rise – Lightly oil a clean bowl, place the dough inside, cover with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let rise 1‑1½ h at room temperature, or until doubled in size.
Punch & divide – Deflate gently, split into 2 equal balls (or 3 for smaller pizzas). Shape each into a tight round, cover, and rest 15 min – this relaxes the gluten for easier stretching.
2. Sauce (quick & flavorful)
Ingredient	Amount
Canned whole‑peeled tomatoes (San Marzano)	400 g (1 can)
Extra‑virgin olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Fresh garlic, minced	2 cloves
Dried oregano	½ tsp
Dried basil	½ tsp
Pinch of sugar (optional)	–
Salt & pepper	to taste
Fresh basil leaves (optional)	a few, torn
Method
Blend – Crush the tomatoes by hand or pulse briefly in a food processor; keep a slightly chunky texture.
Season – Stir in olive oil, garlic, oregano, basil, sugar, salt, and pepper. Taste and adjust. No need to cook; the sauce will finish in the oven, preserving bright tomato flavor.
3. Toppings (keep it simple)
Cheese: Fresh mozzarella (sliced or torn) – 150‑200 g per pizza.
Optional extras: Pepperoni, sliced mushrooms, roasted red peppers, caramelized onions, fresh arugula, drizzle of olive oil, grated Parmesan.
Tip: If using watery mozzarella, pat dry with paper towels to avoid soggy crust.
4. Baking
Parameter	Recommendation
Oven type	Conventional or convection (no pizza stone required, but a stone or steel gives the best crust).
Pre‑heat temperature	250 °C (482 °F) – highest setting your oven allows (most home ovens max at 250 °C).
Pre‑heat time	At least 30 min with the stone/steel inside.
Baking surface	Pizza stone, steel, or upside‑down baking sheet.
Bake time	8‑12 min – watch for a golden‑brown crust and bubbling cheese.
Broiler	If you like extra char, switch to broiler for the last 1‑2 min (watch closely).
Procedure
Stretch dough – On a well‑floured surface, gently stretch each ball into a 12‑inch circle, leaving a slightly thicker edge for the crust.
Add sauce – Spoon ¼‑⅓ cup of sauce, spreading thinly, leaving the rim untouched.
Cheese & toppings – Distribute mozzarella evenly, then add desired toppings.
Slide onto stone – Use a well‑floured pizza peel (or the back of a baking sheet) to transfer the pizza.
Bake – Slide onto the pre‑heated stone/steel. Bake 8‑12 min, rotating halfway for even browning.
Finish – Remove, drizzle a little olive oil, sprinkle fresh basil or Parmesan, and let rest 2 min before slicing.
5. Practical Tips for a Great Home Pizza
Tip	Why it helps
Use high‑protein flour (bread flour or “00” pizza flour)	Gives stronger gluten, yielding a chewy yet airy crust.

Homemade Pizza at Home (Simple, Reliable Method)
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12–14" pizzas)
Ingredients (basic no-knead or quick version):
500 g (4 cups) bread flour or all-purpose flour
300–325 ml (1¼–1⅓ cups) lukewarm water
2 tsp instant yeast (or 7 g packet)
2 tsp salt
2 tbsp olive oil
Quick steps:
Mix dry ingredients (flour, yeast, salt).
Add water + olive oil. Stir until shaggy dough forms (no heavy kneading needed).
Cover and let rise 1–2 hours at room temp (or overnight in fridge for better flavor).
Divide into 2 balls, rest 15–20 min, then stretch by hand into thin rounds (don't use rolling pin if possible — it deflates air pockets).
Tip: For crispier crust, use bread flour and let dough cold-ferment in fridge 24–48 hrs.
2. Sauce (simple classic)
1 can (400 g / 14 oz) crushed tomatoes or passata
1–2 garlic cloves, minced
1 tsp dried oregano
½ tsp salt
Pinch of sugar + black pepper
Optional: fresh basil, red pepper flakes
Mix everything cold — no cooking needed for fresh flavor. Spread thinly (too much sauce makes soggy pizza).
3. Assembly & Toppings
Preheat oven as hot as it goes (ideally 475–550°F / 245–290°C). Use a pizza stone or steel if you have one; otherwise a heavy baking sheet flipped upside-down.
Stretch dough on floured parchment or semolina.
Add thin layer of sauce, then cheese (fresh mozzarella torn into pieces + low-moisture mozzarella for melt), then toppings.
Practical tip: Less is more — overload = soggy. Add delicate items (basil, prosciutto) after baking.
4. Baking
Temperature: 475–550°F (245–290°C) — the hotter the better for home ovens.
Timing:
With pizza stone/steel: 8–12 minutes (rotate halfway).
On regular baking sheet: 12–15 minutes.
Pizza is done when crust is golden-brown with some char spots and cheese is bubbling.
Best home hack: Preheat stone/sheet for 45–60 min at max temp. Launch pizza on parchment for first 5 min, then slide parchment out so bottom crisps directly on stone.
Practical Tips for Success
Crust issues: Too thick? Stretch thinner next time. Soggy bottom? Hotter oven + preheat longer + less sauce.
Cheese: Mix fresh mozzarella (flavor) with low-moisture shredded (less water release).
Oven variation: Gas vs electric matters — watch the first pizza closely and adjust.
Make-ahead: Dough and sauce keep 2–3 days in fridge. Freeze dough balls after first rise.
No stone? Use cast-iron skillet or grill (outdoor grill at high heat gives great results).
Scale toppings to your taste: Margherita (sauce, mozzarella, basil, olive oil drizzle after bake) is the easiest winner.
Start simple with Margherita for your first try — it teaches you the fundamentals fast. Once comfortable, experiment with toppings.
Enjoy your pizza night!

Ingredients 
www.acouplecooks.com
:

Method:
Mix flour, yeast, salt; add water + oil. Stir to shaggy dough.

cookieandkate.com
:

3 garlic cloves, minced

½ tsp dried oregano
½ tsp fine salt

Method: Whisk tomato paste with a splash of crushed tomatoes to loosen, then add remaining ingredients. Taste and adjust. Keeps 5 days refrigerated.

Temperature: 500°F (260°C) is the sweet spot for home ovens

.

Here’s a straightforward, practical guide to make pizza at home with dough, sauce, bake temps, timing, and tips.
Dough (yields 2 medium pizzas)
Ingredients: 2 cups bread flour (or all-purpose), 1 cup warm water (105–110°F/40–43°C), 1 teaspoon instant or active dry yeast, 1 teaspoon sugar, 1 teaspoon salt, 1 tablespoon olive oil.
Method:
Combine water, yeast, and sugar; let sit 5 minutes if using active dry yeast to proof. If using instant yeast, you can mix directly.
Mix in flour and salt, then knead until smooth and elastic (about 6–8 minutes by hand, 4–6 with a mixer). Add a little flour if sticky; add a touch of water if dry.
Coat dough with a little oil, cover, and rise 60–90 minutes at room temp, or until doubled.
Divide into 2 balls, flatten into discs, and let rest 15–20 minutes before stretching.
Tips: For a crispier crust, use bread flour or a 60–40 mix with whole-wheat, and aim for a dough hydration around 65–70%.
Pizza Sauce (quick no-cook version)
Ingredients: 1 can (14–15 oz) crushed tomatoes or tomato puree, 1–2 cloves garlic (minced) or 1 teaspoon garlic powder, 1–2 tablespoons olive oil, 1 teaspoon dried oregano, ½ teaspoon dried basil, ½ teaspoon sugar (optional), salt and pepper to taste.
Method:
Blend or whisk together tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, oregano, basil, sugar, and seasonings until smooth.
Adjust with salt, pepper, or a touch more olive oil to taste.
Quick no-cook note: If you prefer, blend tomatoes with a small amount of olive oil and seasonings; spread thinly on dough if you’re avoiding pre-cooking.
Tips: Use a thin layer of sauce to prevent soggy crust; sauce can be stored in the fridge 3–5 days or frozen for later.
Bake temperature and timing
Home oven (conventional): Preheat to 475–500°F (245–260°C). Bake 10–12 minutes for a thin crust, 12–15 minutes for a standard crust, until edges are golden and cheese is bubbling.
Pizza stone or steel: Place in the cold oven and preheat with the stone/steel inside for 30–45 minutes, then slide pizza onto it for crisper bottom crust.
For very hot setups (optional): If you have a grill or a dedicated pizza oven that reaches 600–900°F, cooking times drop to 2–6 minutes depending on thickness.
Tips: If crust browns too quickly, reduce oven temperature by 25°F and bake a bit longer; if underdone, give it a few more minutes.
Topping assembly
Base: Stretch or roll dough to 12-inch (30 cm) circle; transfer to a floured peel or parchment on a baking surface.
Sauce: Spread a thin layer (1–2 tablespoons per 12-inch dough) to avoid soggy crust.
Cheese: Classic mozzarella (shredded or sliced) or a blend with provolone; sprinkle salt sparingly.
Extras: Pepperoni, vegetables, mushrooms, olives, fresh basil, and a light drizzle of olive oil after baking.
Practical tips
Hydration: Aim for a dough that’s tacky but not sticky; adjust flour by 1–2 tablespoons as needed.
Kneading: Proper gluten development yields a chewy crust; you should be able to poke dough and have it slowly spring back.
Resting: A 15–20 minute bench rest helps dough relax and stretch better.
Pre-bake test: For thick-crust pies, you can par-bake dough 4–5 minutes before adding sauce and toppings to prevent sogginess.
Storage: On dough: refrigerate for up to 3 days or freeze for longer. Sauce can be made in larger batches and frozen.

Homemade Pizza – Simple Step‑by‑Step Guide
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12‑inch pizzas)
Ingredient	Amount
Bread‑flour (high‑protein)	500 g (≈ 4 cups)
Water (lukewarm, ~38 °C)	320 ml (≈ 1 ⅓ cups)
Active‑dry yeast	5 g (1 tsp)
Fine sea salt	10 g (2 tsp)
Olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Optional: a pinch of sugar	2 g (½ tsp)
Method
Activate yeast – In a small bowl, dissolve the yeast (and sugar, if using) in the warm water. Let sit 5 min until foamy.
Mix dry – In a large bowl, whisk flour and salt together.
Combine – Make a well in the flour, pour in the yeast mixture and olive oil. Stir with a wooden spoon until a shaggy dough forms.
Knead – Turn onto a lightly floured surface and knead 8‑10 min (or 5 min in a stand‑mix with a dough hook on medium speed) until smooth, elastic, and slightly tacky.
First rise – Lightly oil a clean bowl, place the dough inside, cover with plastic wrap or a damp towel. Let rise 1‑1½ h at room temperature, or until doubled in size.
Punch & divide – Deflate gently, split into 2 equal balls (or 3 for smaller pizzas). Shape each into a tight round, cover, and rest 15 min – this relaxes the gluten for easier stretching.
2. Sauce (quick & flavorful)
Ingredient	Amount
Canned whole‑peeled tomatoes (San Marzano)	400 g (1 can)
Extra‑virgin olive oil	15 ml (1 tbsp)
Fresh garlic, minced	2 cloves
Dried oregano	½ tsp
Dried basil	½ tsp
Pinch of sugar (optional)	–
Salt & pepper	to taste
Fresh basil leaves (optional)	a few, torn
Method
Blend – Crush the tomatoes by hand or pulse briefly in a food processor; keep a slightly chunky texture.
Season – Stir in olive oil, garlic, oregano, basil, sugar, salt, and pepper. Taste and adjust. No need to cook; the sauce will finish in the oven, preserving bright tomato flavor.
3. Toppings (keep it simple)
Cheese: Fresh mozzarella (sliced or torn) – 150‑200 g per pizza.
Optional extras: Pepperoni, sliced mushrooms, roasted red peppers, caramelized onions, fresh arugula, drizzle of olive oil, grated Parmesan.
Tip: If using watery mozzarella, pat dry with paper towels to avoid soggy crust.
4. Baking
Parameter	Recommendation
Oven type	Conventional or convection (no pizza stone required, but a stone or steel gives the best crust).
Pre‑heat temperature	250 °C (482 °F) – highest setting your oven allows (most home ovens max at 250 °C).
Pre‑heat time	At least 30 min with the stone/steel inside.
Baking surface	Pizza stone, steel, or upside‑down baking sheet.
Bake time	8‑12 min – watch for a golden‑brown crust and bubbling cheese.
Broiler	If you like extra char, switch to broiler for the last 1‑2 min (watch closely).
Procedure
Stretch dough – On a well‑floured surface, gently stretch each ball into a 12‑inch circle, leaving a slightly thicker edge for the crust.
Add sauce – Spoon ¼‑⅓ cup of sauce, spreading thinly, leaving the rim untouched.
Cheese & toppings – Distribute mozzarella evenly, then add desired toppings.
Slide onto stone – Use a well‑floured pizza peel (or the back of a baking sheet) to transfer the pizza.
Bake – Slide onto the pre‑heated stone/steel. Bake 8‑12 min, rotating halfway for even browning.
Finish – Remove, drizzle a little olive oil, sprinkle fresh basil or Parmesan, and let rest 2 min before slicing.
5. Practical Tips for a Great Home Pizza
Tip	Why it helps
Use high‑protein flour (bread flour or “00” pizza flour)	Gives stronger gluten, yielding a chewy yet airy crust.

Homemade Pizza at Home (Simple, Reliable Method)
1. Dough (makes 2 × 12–14" pizzas)
Ingredients (basic no-knead or quick version):
500 g (4 cups) bread flour or all-purpose flour
300–325 ml (1¼–1⅓ cups) lukewarm water
2 tsp instant yeast (or 7 g packet)
2 tsp salt
2 tbsp olive oil
Quick steps:
Mix dry ingredients (flour, yeast, salt).
Add water + olive oil. Stir until shaggy dough forms (no heavy kneading needed).
Cover and let rise 1–2 hours at room temp (or overnight in fridge for better flavor).
Divide into 2 balls, rest 15–20 min, then stretch by hand into thin rounds (don't use rolling pin if possible — it deflates air pockets).
Tip: For crispier crust, use bread flour and let dough cold-ferment in fridge 24–48 hrs.
2. Sauce (simple classic)
1 can (400 g / 14 oz) crushed tomatoes or passata
1–2 garlic cloves, minced
1 tsp dried oregano
½ tsp salt
Pinch of sugar + black pepper
Optional: fresh basil, red pepper flakes
Mix everything cold — no cooking needed for fresh flavor. Spread thinly (too much sauce makes soggy pizza).
3. Assembly & Toppings
Preheat oven as hot as it goes (ideally 475–550°F / 245–290°C). Use a pizza stone or steel if you have one; otherwise a heavy baking sheet flipped upside-down.
Stretch dough on floured parchment or semolina.
Add thin layer of sauce, then cheese (fresh mozzarella torn into pieces + low-moisture mozzarella for melt), then toppings.
Practical tip: Less is more — overload = soggy. Add delicate items (basil, prosciutto) after baking.
4. Baking
Temperature: 475–550°F (245–290°C) — the hotter the better for home ovens.
Timing:
With pizza stone/steel: 8–12 minutes (rotate halfway).
On regular baking sheet: 12–15 minutes.
Pizza is done when crust is golden-brown with some char spots and cheese is bubbling.
Best home hack: Preheat stone/sheet for 45–60 min at max temp. Launch pizza on parchment for first 5 min, then slide parchment out so bottom crisps directly on stone.
Practical Tips for Success
Crust issues: Too thick? Stretch thinner next time. Soggy bottom? Hotter oven + preheat longer + less sauce.
Cheese: Mix fresh mozzarella (flavor) with low-moisture shredded (less water release).
Oven variation: Gas vs electric matters — watch the first pizza closely and adjust.
Make-ahead: Dough and sauce keep 2–3 days in fridge. Freeze dough balls after first rise.
No stone? Use cast-iron skillet or grill (outdoor grill at high heat gives great results).
Scale toppings to your taste: Margherita (sauce, mozzarella, basil, olive oil drizzle after bake) is the easiest winner.
Start simple with Margherita for your first try — it teaches you the fundamentals fast. Once comfortable, experiment with toppings.
Enjoy your pizza night!

Mix in flour and salt, then knead until smooth and elastic (about 6–8 minutes by hand, 4–6 with a mixer). Add a little flour if sticky; add a touch of water if dry.
Coat dough with a little oil, cover, and rise 60–90 minutes at room temp, or until doubled.
Divide into 2 balls, flatten into discs, and let rest 15–20 minutes before stretching.
Tips: For a crispier crust, use bread flour or a 60–40 mix with whole-wheat, and aim for a dough hydration around 65–70%.
Pizza Sauce (quick no-cook version)
Ingredients: 1 can (14–15 oz) crushed tomatoes or tomato puree, 1–2 cloves garlic (minced) or 1 teaspoon garlic powder, 1–2 tablespoons olive oil, 1 teaspoon dried oregano, ½ teaspoon dried basil, ½ teaspoon sugar (optional), salt and pepper to taste.
Method:
Blend or whisk together tomatoes, garlic, olive oil, oregano, basil, sugar, and seasonings until smooth.
Adjust with salt, pepper, or a touch more olive oil to taste.

Kneading: Proper gluten development yields a chewy crust; you should be able to poke dough and have it slowly spring back.
Resting: A 15–20 minute bench rest helps dough relax and stretch better.
